data.title: Ecuador says ICE officer tried to enter consulate in Minneapolis
data.description:
The Ecuadorian Ministry of Foreign Affairs said Tuesday that an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer attempted to enter the South American nation’s consulate in Minneapolis.

The ministry wrote on social platform X that the officer attempted to gain entry to the facility at roughly 11 a.m. CST. Consulate officials “immediately” prevented the immigration official from entering, thereby “ensuring the protection of the Ecuadorians who were at the headquarters at that moment and activating the emergency protocols issued by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Human Mobility.”

The officials also noted the nation’s government presented a “note of protest” to the U.S. Embassy in Quito, Ecuador. 

The Hill has reached out to ICE, the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) and the State Department for comment.

The incident comes amid the Trump administration’s immigration enforcement operation in Minneapolis. The administration surged ICE and Border Patrol personnel to the city earlier this month.

Local and state officials have pushed back against the crackdown in the wake of the fatal shootings of Renee Good by an ICE officer on Jan. 7 and Alex Pretti by Border Patrol agents on Saturday. Both were 37-year-old U.S. citizens. 

White House border czar Tom Homan, who President Trump sent to Minneapolis on Monday to oversee the continued operation after U.S. Border Patrol commander Greg Bovino departed the area, met with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) on Tuesday.

“Governor Walz met with Tom Homan this morning and reiterated Minnesota’s priorities: impartial investigations into the Minneapolis shootings involving federal agents, a swift, significant reduction in the number of federal forces in Minnesota, and an end to the campaign of retribution against Minnesota,” Walz’s office said in a statement to NewsNation, The Hill’s sister network. 

“The Governor and Homan agreed on the need for an ongoing dialogue and will continue working toward those goals, which the President also agreed to yesterday,” his office added. ” The Governor tasked the Minnesota Department of Public Safety as the primary liaison to Homan to ensure these goals are met.”

Trump has largely defended his administration’s moves in the North Star State, despite rising criticism of DHS Secretary Kristi Noem and the department’s use of force in the state. Democrats have threatened to launch impeachment proceedings against Noem if she is not removed or agrees to resign.

data.title: Trump tells Iran 'time is running out' for deal: 'Next attack will be far worse'
data.description:
President Trump on Wednesday threatened Iran with another attack that would be “far worse” than the three strikes last summer.

“Hopefully Iran will quickly ‘Come to the Table’ and negotiate a fair and equitable deal – NO NUCLEAR WEAPONS – one that is good for all parties. Time is running out, it is truly of the essence!” the president wrote in a Truth Social post.

“As I told Iran once before, MAKE A DEAL! They didn’t, and there was “Operation Midnight Hammer,” a major destruction of Iran. The next attack will be far worse! Don’t make that happen again. Thank you for your attention to this matter!” he added.

Last June, the United States struck three Iranian nuclear sites following lethal strikes led by Israel with hopes of dismantling their nuclear weapons development programs. 

Trump has clashed further with leaders in Tehran by backing anti-government protests in the country, warning that any acts of deadly violence against demonstrators would be met with military intervention.

More than 6,000 individuals have been killed since the Iranian government’s crackdown on the protests began.

“A massive Armada is heading to Iran. It is moving quickly, with great power, enthusiasm, and purpose. It is a larger fleet, headed by the great Aircraft Carrier Abraham Lincoln, than that sent to Venezuela,” the president wrote in the Wednesday post. 

“Like with Venezuela, it is, ready, willing, and able to rapidly fulfill its mission, with speed and violence, if necessary,” he continued.

Countries in the Middle East are bracing for lethal attacks.

data.title: Beyond testosterone: The real crisis in men's health
data.description:
Officials at the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services are signaling that testosterone replacement therapy could soon be the centerpiece of a new government-backed initiative focusing on men’s health.  

There’s no question men face a growing crisis of wellbeing in America, evidenced by high rates of addiction, obesity and chronic disease among other afflictions. But prioritizing testosterone use to combat these problems misses the mark.  

Increasing testosterone in men ignores deeply rooted cultural and social factors at the center of this health emergency. To effect meaningful change, our nation’s public health leaders must take a broader view. The national conversation surrounding men’s health should start where the evidence is clear: Men are dying at a younger age than women, largely from preventable causes.

The U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention found that young men suffer higher rates of heart attacks than women. Research also shows men are more likely to abuse alcohol and develop substance use disorders. Help exists, but the majority of men don’t seek it.  

More than 50 percent of men avoid regular medical screenings. For men of color, the number jumps to more than 60 percent. Men are often in the dark when it comes to their blood pressure and cholesterol levels which can yield early detection of many preventable health issues.  

Instead, many men only seek medical care when something is seriously wrong. Statistics show men have higher rates of hypertension, diabetes and heart disease — conditions that can lurk quietly until it’s too late. 

Men’s mental health is another silent killer. Twenty-five percent of young men struggle with loneliness. And nearly four times as many men die by suicide in America than women — yet men are less likely to reach out for help. Millions of men also suffer from undiagnosed depression and anxiety that can lead to unhealthy and destructive behaviors.  

These problems aren’t the result of lower testosterone levels — stigma associated with seeking help is often to blame. Many men are burdened by what the American Heart Association calls “misguided masculinity” — the notion that a man’s vulnerability can be seen or perceived as a sign of weakness.  

From an early age, boys are socialized to “tough it out” if they get hurt — a lesson that can translate into adult behaviors of ignoring pain, suppressing emotion and avoiding doctors to receive the help they need. 

To improve men’s health, we need to normalize mental health care for men and embed it in our schools, workplaces and communities. An investment in mental health programs would have a much bigger impact on men’s health than a wave of testosterone prescriptions.  

Preventative medicine is often discussed in the context of women’s health: Pap smears, mammograms, reproductive health screenings, immunizations and so forth. But men, too, need prevention-based care to encourage early diagnosis of potential deadly conditions. 

Ignoring one’s health and mental wellbeing has consequences. The data prove it. Life expectancy for men in the U.S. has declined in recent years, driven by so-called “deaths of despair” and other illnesses. A national men’s health agenda should focus on promoting health literacy and the importance of seeking care rather than looking to pharmaceuticals to provide simple answers to complex issues. 

Testosterone replacement therapy can be a legitimate medical treatment, especially for men with clinically low hormone levels when administered under physician supervision. But it’s not a cure-all remedy for the vast majority of health issues most men battle every day.  

We don’t need our public health institutions reinforcing the myth that men are simply in need of a chemical rebalancing. Improving men’s health requires that we redefine what strength looks like.

Men need to understand that stoicism isn’t a strategy when it comes to medical care. But this can’t happen unless we address the feelings of shame many men inherently feel when it comes to seeking medical attention. 

Rather than focus on testosterone, we need federally funded programs that promote preventative screenings and encourage men to talk openly about stress, trauma and mental health — and the importance of receiving treatment.   

Breast cancer awareness campaigns normalized open conversations for women and saved lives. These efforts were built on a commitment to change the narrative and remove the stigma that was once synonymous with a serious disease affecting women’s health. We need to approach men’s health issues the same way.  

The key to solving the men’s health crisis in America won’t come in a vial. It will take courage and resolve by those leading U.S. health policy to focus on the urgent work that must be done to tackle the real issues at stake. 

Lyndon Haviland, DrPH, MPH, is a distinguished scholar at the CUNY School of Public Health and Health Policy.

data.title: Watch live: Trump, Bessent host summit on newborn baby accounts
data.description:
President Trump and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent are holding a summit Wednesday morning with CEOs and investors ahead of the administration’s launch of “Trump accounts” for newborn babies.

Under the investment accounts, children in the U.S. with a Social Security number born between Jan. 1, 2025, and Dec. 31, 2028, will be given a stock market account with a one-time $1,000 deposit from the Treasury that will grow similarly to individual retirement accounts, or IRAs, according to the IRS.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt, rapper Nicki Minaj, Sen. Ted Cruz (R-Texas), Rep. Jason Smith (R-Mo.) and Cheryl Hines, the wife of Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr., are set to attend.

The event is scheduled to begin at 9:45 a.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Border Patrol shoots human trafficking suspect in Arizona
data.description:
A man accused of being involved in a human trafficking operation was shot and injured by a U.S. Border Patrol agent Tuesday near the U.S.-Mexico border in Arizona, according to authorities.

Pima County Sheriff Chris Nanos said agents attempted to stop a truck near Arivaca, Ariz., about 10 miles north of the border, around 7:20 a.m. but the driver did not stop and fled.

“The agent, the Border Patrol agent chased after him on foot. Shortly thereafter, shots were fired from the suspect and the agent returned fire,” Nanos said during a news conference.

The FBI identified the suspect as Patrick Gary Schlegel, a 34-year-old Arizona resident.

As agents were pursuing him, Schlegel fired at a manned Customs and Border Protection (CBP) helicopter that was flying above, said Heith Janke, the special agent in charge of the FBI Phoenix field office.

Schlegel was injured and taken to the hospital, where officials said Tuesday he was in “serious and stable condition.” No injuries to law enforcement officers were reported.

He was taken into federal custody and is expected to face three criminal charges, according to Janke: assault on a federal officer, alien smuggling, and felon in possession of a firearm.

Officials said Schlegel has a “significant criminal history,” including an active federal arrest warrant issued last year for escape related to a previous “federal alien smuggling conviction.”

Federal court records show that Schlegel was sentenced in 2024 to 36 months in prison after being convicted of transportation of illegal aliens for profit and felony gun possession charges.

A criminal complaint states he was serving some of his sentence at a re-entry institution in Tucson, Ariz., when he escaped. He reportedly never returned after signing out of the facility on Dec. 15 for an alcohol evaluation and counseling.

The Department of Homeland Security confirmed in a statement to The Hill’s sister network, NewsNation, that Tuesday’s shooting is being investigated by the CBP Office of Professional Responsibility, the FBI and Pima County Sheriff’s Department.

“We would ask for the community’s patience in allowing the investigative and prosecutor process to continue. Let me be clear, any assault on law enforcement officers will not be tolerated,” Janke said Tuesday.

The shooting in Arizona came days after U.S. Border Patrol agents shot and killed a 37-year-old Alex Pretti in Minneapolis, about a block from where Renee Good, also 37, was fatally shot in her SUV by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer.

data.title: Trump: Belichick falling short in Hall of Fame vote 'ridiculous'
data.description:
President Trump is expressing outrage at a decision by the Pro Football Hall of Fame not to nominate legendary coach Bill Belichick for induction the first year he was eligible for selection.

“It is the same mindset that gave pro football the new and unwatchable ‘Sissy’ Kickoff Rule, that made it possible for Bill Belichick to not be elected into the Pro Football Hall of Fame,” Trump wrote Wednesday on his Truth Social platform. “Both are ridiculous and should be overturned!”

Belichick, who won six Super Bowls with the New England Patriots and built a dynasty there, is widely considered one of the greatest coaches in NFL history.

ESPN reported on Tuesday that Belichick had failed to secure the 40 votes necessary from the Hall of Fame selection committee — made up of media members and inducted former players and coaches — to win selection for enshrinement in Canton, Ohio.

The news sparked widespread outrage across the NFL, and most sharply among fans of the Patriots, who are gearing up for another Super Bowl on Feb. 8 against the Seattle Seahawks.

Trump has ridiculed the NFL in recent years on issues ranging from its kickoff rule to initiatives on social justice.

data.title: Only Trump can defuse the powder keg in Minneapolis
data.description:
Two human beings are dead. Two families must now endure the crippling grieving process. Thousands of federal officers are on the razor’s edge as they fear their safety. Tens of thousands of their family members now constantly fear for the physical and mental wellbeing of their loved ones serving on the front lines of an ever-escalating immigration battle.   

It’s time to address the elephant on the ICE. 

President Trump has rightfully taken issue with the editorial page of The Wall Street Journal from time to time. That said, that page also advocates for commonsense thinking that can simultaneously benefit the president and his political party. A recent editorial by the Journal called for “ICE to Pause in Minneapolis.” Every single Republican and conservative I have spoken with in the last few days agrees.

Rep. James Comer (R-Ky.) — one of Trump’s strongest supporters in Congress — suggested this Sunday to Maria Bartiromo on Fox News’s “Sunday Morning Futures” that the president might want to consider pulling Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers out of Minnesota, a day after a Border Patrol agent was involved in a fatal shooting in Minneapolis.  Said Comer, in part: “I think this situation’s only going to get worse, and I really think that there are so many cities in the United States that have issues with criminal illegals, that I think [federal immigration enforcement] would be better received in other cities.”

Bill Melugin of Fox News posted on X: “I’ve talked to more than half a dozen federal sources involved immigration enforcement, including several in senior positions, who all tell me they have grown increasingly uneasy & frustrated w/ some of the claims & narratives DHS pushed in the aftermath of the shooting” — those “narratives” being that Alex Pretti was intending to conduct a “massacre” of federal agents or was a “domestic terrorist.” 

While making an appearance on “Fox abd Friends,” Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche pointed out that Pretti’s actions before he was fatally shot on Saturday did not constitute domestic terrorism. Said Blanche: “I don’t think anybody thinks that they were comparing what happened on Saturday to the legal definition of domestic terrorism. What we saw was a very violent altercation, and I am not going to prejudge the facts.” 

Those same Republicans and conservatives I have spoken with have also long believed that Trump would be better served if highly respected and decorated Hispanic law enforcement officials were the “face” articulating the administration’s immigration policies. This matters more now than ever, especially if the Republican Party is going to be bleeding Hispanic votes because of this controversy.

Trump was right to call on Gov. Tim Walz (D), Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D), and “EVERY Democrat Governor and Mayor in the United States of America to formally cooperate with the Trump Administration to enforce our Nation’s Laws, rather than resist and stoke the flames of Division, Chaos, and Violence.” That said, he must recognize that a fuse leading toward a massive powder keg has been lit, and must be yanked out as quickly as possible.  

The last time I checked, that was called “leadership.”

The major reason for Trump’s success is that he brings more real-world experience into the Oval Office than any president has in our lifetimes. Business experience has made him a master negotiator who knows how and when to redirect a battle before it devolves. And thankfully, Trump appears willing and able to yank that fuse out before the powder keg blows.  

On Monday, the president reported that he had spoken with the state’s governor over the phone, and that they “seemed to be on a similar wavelength” with respect to the unfolding turmoil. It seems near certain that Trump is about to course-correct his immigration policy, which is still strongly supported by the vast majority of Americans. By doing so, he can save the legacy of his administration, and his party’s chances in November’s midterms. 

Now that’s real leadership. 

Douglas MacKinnon is a former White House and Pentagon official.

data.title: Live updates: Senate tackles funding package amid DHS furor; Rubio testifies on Venezuela
data.description:
The Senate on Wednesday will work to advance a six-bill spending package, despite calls from Democrats to separate out Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding after the deadly weekend shooting of a U.S. citizen in Minneapolis by federal immigration officials.

The incident has put Republicans on defense, and they are starting to splinter over support for DHS Secretary Kristi Noem. She retains the confidence of President Trump, however.

Overnight, Minnesota Rep. Ilhan Omar (D), who represents Minneapolis and some of its suburbs, was attacked at town hall in the city, as she called for Noem’s impeachment.

This morning, Secretary of State Marco Rubio is testifying in Congress on the administration’s actions in Venezuela. At 11 a.m., Trump will speak at a Treasury Department summit on his investment accounts for newborn babies.

Later, the Federal Reserve will announce its interest rate decision and chair Jerome Powell will deliver remarks. The central bank is expected to keep rates steady.

Follow live below.

data.title: Trump endorses challengers to Indiana Republicans who opposed redistricting
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday endorsed challengers to three Indiana GOP senators who had voted against redistricting last month. 

In separate posts on his Truth Social Platform, Trump endorsed Paula Copenhaver, a top aide to Indiana Lt. Gov. Micah Beckwith (R), to run for state Sen. Spencer Deery’s (R) seat. He endorsed Vigo County Councilmember Brenda Wilson to run for state Sen. Greg Goode’s (R) seat, and former state Rep. Jeff Ellington (R) to run for state Sen. Eric Bassler’s (R) seat.

Bassler announced last summer he wouldn’t be seeking reelection.

Deery, Goode and Bassler were among 21 Senate Republicans who voted in December to reject a bid by the party to redraw the state’s congressional map ahead of 2026, drawing ire from the president and his allies as they sought to pick up two seats in the House from the Hoosier State. 

“‘No Goode’ is an incompetent and ineffective RINO incumbent who, for whatever reason, voted against Redistricting in Indiana, which has made him a hero to Hakeem Jeffries, Ilhan Omar, and every other Radical Left Member of the U.S. House of Representatives,” Trump said in one of his Truth Social posts, referring to Goode, who also serves as the state director for U.S. Sen. Todd Young (R-Ind.). 

Goode suggested in a social media post that Wilson had encouraged him against voting for redistricting in October. 

“I was grateful for her advice in October: ‘Greg, DON’T vote for redistricting!’” Goode wrote on X. 

Trump vowed to endorse primary challengers to any Republicans who voted against redrawing a new House map in the state. He endorsed a primary challenger to state Sen. Jim Buck (R) on Monday, too, who is running for another term.

data.title: Walz: 'Noem still having a job today is absolutely outrageous'
data.description:
Gov. Tim Walz (D) on Tuesday said it was “absolutely outrageous” for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to still have a job after two fatal shootings in Minnesota involving immigration authorities. 

Both Democrats and Republicans have called on President Trump to fire Noem for backing immigration officials who shot and killed Renee Good and Alex Pretti, calling each a “domestic terrorist,” ahead of an investigation. 

“It‘s insane. And I appreciate all the news media here telling it and tell the rest of the country, ‘Stand with us on this.’ This is a stand we have to hold on. They have to leave here,” Walz said during a Tuesday appearance on CNN’s “AC360.”

“They have to bring these folks to justice. And there has to be some accountability to the top levels. Kristi Noem still having a job today is absolutely outrageous,” he added.

Amid the clamor, the president has defended the Homeland Security secretary for her work at the border.

“I think she’s doing a very good job. The border is totally secure. You know, you forget we had a border that I inherited where millions of people were coming through. Now we have a border where no one is coming through. They come into our country only legally,” Trump told reporters on Tuesday.

“As soon as you accomplish something, it goes into history and nobody ever wants to talk about it,” he continued. 

However, Walz pushed back on Trump’s support for her, telling Anderson Cooper, “it’s very disheartening to me to see the president say Kristi Noem is doing a great job. 

“I‘m not sure there’s been anybody more incompetent or cruel that’s ever served in a cabinet position than her, and this is on her,” he added.

The embattled Minnesota governor said the Trump administration is “undermining order” and fueling “chaos.”

He called for the men responsible for shooting Good and Pretti to be held accountable.

Walz said, “We need to know who these people were. We need these guys brought back here.”

“We have to keep order and its very difficult when they are undermining order. They create this chaos and we have to come in and clean it up.

In recent days, Walz deployed the Minnesota National Guard to work alongside local police in Minneapolis. He noted the importance of soldiers wearing ”uniforms on with their names and no masks” after previously criticizing federal immigration officers for hiding their identity.
